text: This presentation is for the single port robotic transanal minimally invasive surgery or SB-TAMIS technique.
We have no disclosures.
The first case will demonstrate the basic principles of this technique.
And the second case will demonstrate the capabilities of this technique.

The first case is a fifty nine year old male with unresectable polyp on screening colonoscopy. This was a three centimeter tubular villus adenoma, sixteen centimeters from the anal verge. who otherwise had normal imaging.
The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position with the single port robotic system docked between the legs, preparing for the transanal minimally invasive surgery.
A gel port is inserted to protect the anal canal, and a single port trocar is docked. An assistant port is also connected to the continuous CO₂ insufflation system.
The surgeon examines the lesion using the robotic camera, with an assist port available for introducing tools like suction into the surgical field.
The robotic arms are docked under direct visualization, with their movements tracked by an animation displayed at the bottom of the screen.
The surgeon uses monopolar electrocautery to mark the boundaries of resection on the tissue, creating visible cautery lines around the lesion.
The surgeon performs a full thickness dissection, revealing the perirectal fat layer.
The surgeon uses the assist port to introduce instruments that provide counter traction to stabilize the floppy rectum during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects out the flap of rectum, following the marked boundaries established at the beginning of the procedure, after achieving the necessary depth of dissection.
It is important to check frequently if the proximal margin has been reached to avoid taking more rectum than needed, which would make the closure of this defect more challenging.
The assistant grasps the completed specimen to maintain its orientation and removes it through the robotic port.
The surgeon begins closure of the rectal defect using two barbed absorbable sutures, starting at both ends and proceeding transversely to maintain the rectal lumen's width.

You can see from the animation on the bottom of the screen that the robotic arms are fully deployed and functioning very analogous to the traditional robotic TAMIS tools in this capacity. If the arms experience any external tension, the color of the arms change from white to yellow and eventually to red.
We will see the relevance of this in the second case to be presented.
The assistant cuts and removes the sutures, followed by a completion proctoscopy that visually confirms the defect has been sutured closed.
This picture shows comparison to what it looked like prior to the operation. And this marks the completion of this case.

For the second case, we present a fifty-year-old female with large endoscopically unresectable mid-rectal sessile mass that is five centimeters in size and tubular villus adenoma, who also had benign imaging.
The video shows a sessile mass located closer to the dentate line, with the borders of the trans-anal gel port clearly visible during the procedure.
The surgical video shows the red color of instrument animation at the bottom of the screen, indicating that the robotic arms are not fully deployed due to restriction by the gel port canal.
Functionally, this means the arms have a smaller operative area to work with. But by moving the camera and making small movements, we're able to redirect this area and still perform the operation.
Notice the platform allows for rotation of a perceived top-down view even though we are operating on anterior lesion as the water is pooling above us.
The camera moves in a serpiginous motion to enhance proximal visualization of the rectal tumor, overcoming the obstruction in the line of view caused by the tumor.
The surgeon begins full thickness dissection of the rectum after marking the margins, using the trans-anal gel port to access the area near the dentate line.
The distal dissection close to the anal verge is the most challenging part of this procedure, but not impossible.
The robotic arms operate with precision in a confined area, while the surgeon adjusts the camera view to enhance visibility and maneuverability.
The robotic choke arm moves as a whole, redirecting the surgical field to adjust the area of focus.
The robotic arms are fully deployed and operating normally in the proximal rectum. The animation at the bottom of the screen shows the arms in green, indicating successful deployment.
The surgeon checks the proximal margin of the rectum during the dissection to ensure the sample size remains appropriate, preventing the defect from enlarging.
The surgeon uses the assist port to remove the fully dissected mass, carefully maintaining its orientation for potential frozen section analysis.
The surgeon uses two barbed absorbable sutures from both ends to close a large defect transversely, demonstrating the closure technique on the visible defect.

It cannot be stressed enough the importance of continuous CO₂ insufflation instead of the traditional pulsatile CO₂ insufflation during this case to prevent the bellowing of the colon at this very critical time.
The camera shows a completion view of the closed defect, indicating the end of the surgical procedure.

Both patients did very well postoperatively. We performed this operation as an outpatient procedure and they are discharged postoperative day zero. Both had favorable pathology and were seen as three months postoperatively with regular bowel function.
